question: I _____ (see) that new superhero movie yesterday.
explanation: We use 'saw' because the word 'yesterday' gives us a specific, finished time in the past.

question: She _____ (already / finish) her math homework.
explanation: We use 'has already finished' because 'already' shows an action completed at an unspecified time before now.

question: _____ you ever _____ (eat) sushi?
explanation: We use 'Have eaten' because the word 'ever' asks about your life experience up to this moment.

question: My family _____ (go) to the beach last weekend.
explanation: We use 'went' because 'last weekend' is a specific time period that is completely finished.

question: He _____ (live) in this town since he was a baby.
explanation: We use 'has lived' because the word 'since' tells us the action started in the past and continues to the present.

question: They _____ (buy) a new car in 2020.
explanation: We use 'bought' because 'in 2020' is a specific, finished point in the past.

question: I _____ (not / see) my house keys. Have you?
explanation: We use 'haven't seen' because it is a past action that has a direct result right now (the keys are still missing).

question: William Shakespeare _____ (write) Romeo and Juliet.
explanation: We use 'wrote' because Shakespeare lived in the past. His life is a finished time period, so he cannot write any more books.

question: My favorite author _____ (write) five books so far.
explanation: We use 'has written' because the author is still alive and the phrase 'so far' means she might write more in the future.

question: _____ you _____ (finish) your science project yet?
explanation: We use 'Have finished' because the word 'yet' asks if an expected action has happened before this present moment.

question: I _____ (lose) my phone at the park last night.
explanation: We use 'lost' because 'last night' tells us exactly when it happened.

question: Oh no! I _____ (cut) my finger! It's bleeding.
explanation: We use 'have cut' because it is a recent past action with a very obvious result in the present (it's bleeding now).

question: She _____ (visit) Paris three times in her life.
explanation: We use 'has visited' to talk about life experiences up to the present moment, without giving a specific date.

question: When _____ you _____ (arrive) at the party?
explanation: We use 'did arrive' because questions starting with 'When' are asking for a specific finished time.

question: I _____ (know) my best friend for five years. We hang out every day!
explanation: We use 'have known' because the word 'for' shows a duration that started in the past and is still true today.

question: The train to London _____ (leave) ten minutes ago.
explanation: We use 'left' because the word 'ago' is always used with the Past Simple to show a finished time.

question: We _____ (not / play) tennis this week because of the rain.
explanation: We use 'haven't played' because 'this week' is an unfinished time period; the week isn't over yet!

question: I _____ (never / be) to Australia, but I want to go someday.
explanation: We use 'have never been' because 'never' describes your life experience from birth up to now.

question: He _____ (work) at the bakery for two years, but then he quit to become a teacher.
explanation: We use 'worked' (Past Simple) even though it says 'for two years', because the action is completely finished (he quit).

question: I _____ (just / finish) reading an amazing book! Do you want to borrow it?
explanation: We use 'have just finished' because 'just' indicates an action completed a very short time ago.
